source.title: How Evolution May Work Through Curiosity-Driven Developmental Process

confidence_rationale: Topics in Cognitive Science review + robotic experiment by Oudeyer (FLOWERS team) and Linda Smith (Indiana developmental psychology). The robot experiment showing ordered developmental stages from curiosity-driven learning (first affordances, then vocal interaction) directly evidences that progress-seeking intrinsic motivation is sufficient to generate a structured curriculum without external design. Source quality: venue is strong (TopiCS); it is partly a theoretical synthesis. Mapping fidelity: strong because it directly addresses REE's transition from random exploration to structured action patterns.
failure_signatures: The robot experiment uses a simplified environment; the generalization to REE's reef/harm environment is not guaranteed.

The intrinsic reward signal (learning progress = reduction in prediction error) is one specific implementation; whether REE's existing E1/E2 prediction error signals naturally implement this is an open question.
Ordered stages are a specific prediction that may not hold in REE's continuous action space.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Curiosity-driven learning (maximizing learning progress, operationalized as expected prediction error reduction) self-organizes ordered developmental stages with a mixture of regularities and diversities. The robot learner successively discovers object affordances and vocal interactions without any pre-programmed curriculum.
mapping.ree_translation: For REE's infant stage, the transition from random exploration to structured action patterns does not require an external curriculum signal -- it can emerge from prediction error reduction as an intrinsic reward. This grounds ARC-065 (behavioral diversity generation) in a self-organizing mechanism: the agent naturally transitions to exploitation when learning progress on exploration slows. E1's prediction error is the natural candidate for this signal in REE.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The robot experiment uses a simplified embodiment and environment. REE's harm/goal landscape introduces asymmetric costs that are absent from curiosity-only architectures, so pure curiosity-driven learning would need augmentation with harm sensitivity.
mapping.source_context: Robotic experiment (FLOWERS team, INRIA/ENSTA); developmental psychology synthesis; Topics in Cognitive Science invited contribution.

confidence_components.notes: Strong theoretical + empirical paper; mapping is close but REE's harm dimension adds complexity not present in curiosity-only systems.
